items.headline: How I Fired Myself From My Own Business
items.angle: Frame the story around a specific 'rock bottom' moment caused by hustle culture, where you chose to rebuild your business from scratch using systems so it could run without you, allowing you to 'live offline.'
hooks: It was a Tuesday afternoon in May, and I missed my kid's soccer game for a Zoom call that could have been an email.
I looked at my screen time report. 14 hours. And I realized my business owned me, not the other way around.
My wife asked me when I was last truly present. I didn't have an answer. That's when I shut the laptop.
I had built a six figure business that felt like a minimum wage job. So I decided to burn it down.
On June 1st, I deleted Slack from my phone. It was the first step to getting my life back.
talking_points: It was a Tuesday afternoon in May. I missed my son's soccer game for a Zoom call that absolutely could have been an email.
That was the moment I knew something had to change. I had a huge task in front of me: rebuild my entire business so it could run without me, so I’d never have to make that choice again.
For years, I'd been a prisoner to my own business. I bought the lie that more hours and more hustle meant more success.
But the truth is, it was costing me my health, my relationships, and my presence with my family.

I realized I had two choices. I could keep grinding away on the path to burnout and regret. Or, I could burn the old model to the ground and build something new, something that actually served my life.
So I did. I spent the next 90 days building AI and automation engines into the core of my business. I automated the marketing, the sales, the operations.
And it worked. The business now runs better without my constant attention. I haven't missed a game since.
I get to take weeks off, completely unplugged, and come back to a business that's grown while I was gone.
I didn't get my life back by working harder. I got it back by building smarter systems that do the work for me.
If you're tired of being a prisoner to your business, I put together a roadmap on how I built these systems. Just comment 'FIELDWORK' below and I'll send it over to you.

items.headline: Stop Selling The Grind, Start Selling Freedom
items.angle: Frame the content around the two kinds of businesses you can sell: one that adds more work (the common mistake) versus one that buys back time (the FieldWork way). This mirrors the original's A/B structure but uses your core philosophy.
hooks: Most founders are accidentally selling a second job. Here’s what to sell instead.
There are two types of online businesses you can sell. One traps you, the other sets you free.
Why selling more features, calls, and content attracts the wrong clients.
Are you selling your clients 'More' or are you selling them 'Less'?
Your offer is attracting hustle addicts. Here's how to attract founders who want freedom.
talking_points: There are really only two types of online businesses you can sell to your clients.
The first is the 'More' business. This is what most people sell. It's built on more calls, more access, more templates, more one on one time.

The problem is, this attracts people who are addicted to the hustle. They measure success by hours worked, so they end up buying another job from you, and you end up working that job right alongside them.
It's why adding all those bells and whistles to your offer just leaves everyone feeling burnt out.
The other option is to sell a 'Less' business. Less time chained to a desk, less complexity, less of you as the bottleneck.
You do this by selling the outcome and the systems that create it, not just more of your time.
This is powerful because it attracts a totally different kind of person. Someone who values their time and wants to build an asset that serves their life, not the other way around.
So, lead with the vision of what life looks like offline. Tell the story of building the engines once, so they can run for years.
This isn't about being lazy, it's about being intentional. The work is real, but it's focused on building systems that buy back your time.
If you want to see how we build these kinds of businesses, comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I'll send you our roadmap.

items.headline: You Have a Duty To Disappear
items.angle: Reframe the 'duty to get rich' into a 'duty to get free'. It's not about hustle, but about a moral responsibility to yourself and your family to build a business that doesn't consume your life, allowing you to actually live it.
hooks: You have a duty to get your time back.
You have a moral obligation to automate your business.
You have a responsibility to build a business that runs without you.
You have a duty to your family to not be a slave to your work.
You have a duty to disappear from the day to day operations.
talking_points: You have a duty to get your time back.
And I don't mean that in some hustle culture, 'grind for a Lambo' kind of way. I mean you have a fundamental responsibility to not waste your one life chained to a laptop.
If you've built a successful business but it requires you to be 'on' all the time, you haven't built a business. You've just built yourself a high stress job.
Your duty is to your family, to your passions, to the version of you that exists outside of Slack notifications and email.
The way you honor that duty is by building the engines inside your business that can run without you.
This means using smart automation and AI to handle client intake, marketing, and fulfillment so the business serves you, not the other way around.
It’s about building once, so you can return to the field. So you can take a Tuesday off to go fishing, or a month off to travel, and know that everything is still working.
That is the real work. That is the duty.
If you want to see the roadmap for how we build these freedom engines for founders and experts, just comment the word 'FIELD' below and I'll send it over to you.

items.headline: My Business's Unfair Advantage
items.angle: Frame your AI and automation systems as the 'performance enhancer' for your business. This is the secret that allows you to be 'offline' so much, directly answering the question everyone is thinking.
hooks: Dude, how are you always offline?
What AI are you on, man?
Is your business just running itself now?
Seriously, how much are you actually working?
How do you have a business and a life?
talking_points: I get asked some version of this question almost every day: 'Dude, how are you always offline?'
People see me on trips, with my family, out in the world, and they think I must have a giant team running things.
The truth is, I don't have a huge team. I have systems.
A few years ago, I stopped trading my time for dollars and instead invested my time building a handful of core AI and automation engines.
These engines now handle about 90 percent of the repetitive work that used to burn up all my time.
They do everything from finding and nurturing new leads to onboarding clients into my programs.
It's the foundation of my entire philosophy: Build online, so you can live offline.
It is not magic and it is not about getting lucky. It is about building a different kind of business.
If you want the exact roadmap I used to build these systems for my own business, just comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I will send it to you.
items.body_copy:
I get asked some version of this question all the time. 'Dude, how are you always offline?'

People see me on trips, with my family, out in the world, and they assume I either sold my business or I have some giant team running it for me.

The truth is, I don't. What I have are systems.

A few years ago, I decided to stop trading my time for money and instead invested my time building a handful of core AI and automation engines. These engines now handle about 90% of the repetitive work, from finding and talking to new customers to onboarding new clients.

It's the engine that lets me build online, so I can live offline. It's not magic, it's just a different model.

If you want the roadmap I used to build these systems for my own business, just comment the word 'FieldWork' below and I will send it over to you.
script: Hook with the question you always get: 'How are you always offline?'
Debunk the common assumptions (big team, not working).
Reveal the real answer: It's not people, it's systems.
Explain the 'AI engines' that handle 90% of the repetitive work.
State the core philosophy: 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
CTA: Comment 'FieldWork' for the roadmap.
